author: @stevetobias4890
text: If you want a fast hot fuse use potassium permanganate and drip a couple of drops of vegetable glycerine on the end and walk away, after a couple of seconds the fuse will burn hot and fast. These are two ingredients that are handy in a survival kit, they start a fire very fast.

replies.author: @suzannehartmann946
replies.text: YEAAH permanganate is a little much

author: @JohnCol-1959
text:
Hmmm. Timing the fire burn in still air & strong winds will have different results.
To be on the safe side, repeat the test inside with still air to get a max burn time.
You don't want to be caught returning to a "failed ignition" to find 15 seconds of burn & blow up in your face !!

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
The following information is provided for educational purposes only, not as a recommendation for anyone to make. 

There's number of simple fuses that can be made with  potassium or sodium chlorate salt. News paper soaked in  a strong solution, and dried acts a a fuse material. Absorbent news paper, the unglazed tabloid paper work best. When the paper is dry, wrap and  twisted a length about one inch wide, and place  in a strip of  sellotape,   then  twist the tape tightly.. This produces a medium fast fuse, with a typical burn speed of about an inch per second. Add a few crystals of cobalt chloride to the initial  solution. This acts as an indicator, turning blue when the paper is dry, but more importantly, it acts as a catalyst that greatly speeds the oxidiser decomposition, producing an extremely fast fuse burn, to the point where it can burn with a loud crack, and take off as a mini rocket, if not restrained.
 
Very slow burning touch paper can be made by soaking similar news paper in a solution of potassium permanganate. Adding a very small amount of potassium nitrate can be used to increase the  speed of burn. The pure permanganate paper is similar to the slow burning blue touch paper found on some fireworks.
The potassium permanganate turns the paper dark brown. when dry. It burns with a smoldering flame front, that  is difficult to extinguish. when used in combination with the very fast fuse, it provides a safer way to light it, giving a slow start.
The soaked news paper sheets, are extremely flammable, and must be treated with great respect. The chlorate version burns like nitrocellulose flash paper, and leaves little residue. 
The speed of drying is important, particularly with the potassium nitrate paper. As this controls the crystal growth of the salt trapped in the fibers of the paper. Slow drying produces a slower rate of burn, and can result in beads of molten nitrate salt forming at the flame front.
Forced drying can be dangerous, as the paper will light with the slightest spark. Warm air drying is best, well away from a naked flame. Even drying over an electric hot plate is dangerous, as rising hot dust particles will set off the dry paper. 
The permanganate paper is safe to confine, but both versions of the chlorate paper are low explosives that will burst a container, if ignited, they will  act as a rocket propellent or fuel for a gun.  None are particularly sensitive to friction or impact, but when very dry, friction and static is a risk. The permanganate paper is very safe to handle. Gloves and eye protection is required when handling the solutions.
The chlorates are poisonous and irritate the skin. The permanganate is also an irritant and tends to stain everything it touches, including skin, dark brown.  ( KMnO4 was once used as a artificial sun tan, before safer and more realistic compounds were discovered.)
The fuse wrapped in tape, will actually burn under water once lit. The tape stops the paper from immediately becoming soaked, and the burning front, generates so much gas, it keeps the water away, stopping it entering the body of the fuse. 
None of the above should be made in any quantity, and should be kept in a sealed, light weight, plastic container, to keep it dry. A silica gel desiccator will stop the fuse paper becoming damp. Damp fuse can result in recrystallise of the salt, resulting in a poor burn profile and larger amounts of residue. The sodium chlorate salt is hygroscopic, and will become wet if left exposed to the air. It must be sealed in tape or a container as quickly as possible after drying. The chlorate paper make excellent fuel for bridge wire ignition, as they take a flame so readly. A small section wrapped between the wires of a bridge wire igniter, will act as an electric fuse head, and when sealed in tape, remain stable and viable for years.  
Sodium chlorate, actually works better than potassium chlorate, but is very hygroscopic until sealed in the tape. 
If the tape is not twisted, but simply folded closed, the chlorate fuse will burn very fast. When twisted, it burns more like the viscose fuses fitted to professional fireworks. All the above fuses have a long term stability, provided they are stored dry. 
It worth note that sellotape is porous to water, being made of cellophane. Plastic insulating  tape  will not work, as the combustion products tend to extinguish the flame. Small lengths of plastic tape are useful in making electric fuses, as it is imperious to water. 

In my youth, about 13 years old, I experimented with a number of fuse mixtures, attempting to emulate the fuses shown on in films and on television. Those described above worked very well. I am sure the regulations controlling the chemicals listed are stricter now, and access to industrially made chlorates is controlled. Though, the YouTube channels are full of detail of how chlorates and perchlorates can be made using an electrolytic cell, fueled with common chloride salts. Even all those years ago, access to perchlorate was difficult, but chlorates WERE commonly available as a general purpose path weed killer. Terrorist activity in the intervening years, and concerns about environmental poisoning, means that chlorates are now banned, and cannot be purchased by the general population.

author: @errolmagill1611
text: Good fun video. I always have to convert metric to imperial measurements in my head. I guess that you younger people have been taught nothing else but metric, and old timers like me are a dying breed.

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: It's about 2 inches. I hate converting also but the other way around. No wonder NASA have problems using both metric and imperial systems in the same project.

replies.author: @errolmagill1611
replies.text: @stevetobias4890 yes, I can imagine that causing problems between various contractors. Metric measurements are admittedly more accurate and easier to calculate. 👍

replies.author: @errolmagill1611
replies.text: Rik rik nightmare!  I tend to use metric mostly when I am fabricating thing's. Here in the UK both are used 😣, our distances are still in yards and miles; I do get quite annoyed when a television program is made for the UK audience and distance is shown in metres and kilometres; I can never understand why they do that!!
